comments.text: Hi all, I have also independently attempted to work on this problem using ChatGPT-5.5-Pro. After carefully reviewing the result, I believe it leads to a possible solution, though I would greatly appreciate any comments, corrections, or feedback. (This link has a solution for erdos 521 as well). Very briefly, we establish a uniform fourth-moment concentration estimate for Littlewood polynomials. The argument combines anti-concentration for Rademacher sums, control of small values, a smoothing step, and tools such as Pisier’s inequality and results of Nazarov–Nishry–Sodin. It appears to yield a quantitative almost-sure result with a convergence rate on the order of $n^{7/8+\delta}$ for any $\delta>0$, which I found noteworthy, though this may well not be optimal. Please see the link for more details. As a note, at the time I was working on this problem, I was not aware of Przemek Chojecki’s solution, so I didn't use it as a part of prompts. That said, I do find the outcome quite interesting, in that AI can generate a range of different approaches. In this case, I suspect this is partly because I prompted ChatGPT to address the gap between Yakir’s in-probability convergence result and an almost-sure convergence statement, and the resulting argument ultimately builds on Yakir’s framework. I have also discussed this with Brayden (thanks!), and wanted to share this for any feedback or thoughts.

I've talked with Brayden - and he's also commented below - that he works on a more general paper that would imply the problem, as the other group of people. So with his permission, I'm posting a streamlined and cleaned note that just solves the problem in a fastest way. Here's the note done with GPT-5.5 Pro. I wasn't able to formalize it.

Hello all. There are comments everywhere so I'll just reply with my own post in regards to everything. To be frank, I was hoping/anticipating that I would be able to share the proof-sketch with the anticipation of only communicating the technique behind the proof itself for those who were genuinely interested in the problem and the tool that could be used to solve it (I have only really found interest in the problems in probability on this website, so I have been actively working on them). I have also dabbled on solving other problems here, but I am exclusively reserving the "currently working on this problem" to problems for which I have a definitive solution to, and I am currently pushing the result to be as good as it possibly can. I'm meeting with Marcus Michelen and Oren Yakir about this problem on Friday, they can prove a far more interesting strengthened result which would imply 522 immediately, so this is the only reason why you haven't seen me post a solution, as I don't want to step on their toes, nor have their work step on mine. About solutions, this includes 521, and I emailed Terry about this a couple months back. Terry's ideas are correct and in fact the language becomes much nicer when one passes to understanding 521 by passing to a particular Gaussian process (see 521 for the details). I would like to formally just state here that if Terry, Vjeko, and Quanyu would like to collaborate on writing up this problem I would be quite interested in doing this (send me an email at [email protected]$). I think it would be very interesting to determine the precise $\limsup$ and $\liminf$ of 521, and this might not be impossible. I would really appreciate if one didn't post a solution to 521 without at least sending me an email. I really do exclusively reserve "currently working on this problem" as "actively writing the paper". In any case, I'm done my finals now (phew). I'm meeting with Marcus Michelen and Oren Yakir about this problem on Friday. If we can't find a way to combine our joint works then Marcus and Oren will submit their paper on arXiv and I will share their result. After all, we are interested in pushing the field to its brink rather than simply providing an answer to an old question.
Thanks for writing it, looking forward to the paper(s)!

I've run remarks of Vjeko Kovac and Brayden Letwin through GPT-5.4 Pro and it came up with a claim for a complete solution. There are many analytic estimates, so I'll probably try to formalize it too. The proof combines three ingredients. First, Jensen's formula reduces the root count to logarithmic integrals at radii $1$ and $1-n^{-1-\alpha}$, with an elementary reciprocal-polynomial squeeze handling the closed disk exactly. Second, a logarithmic-integrability theorem of Nazarov, Nishry and Sodin controls the singularity of $\log |P_n|$ near zero. Third, a quantitative central-limit argument for angle averages of the normalized polynomial, in the spirit of Angst and Poly, yields almost-sure convergence of suitable smooth truncations of the logarithmic integral.

I'm wondering: have you been in contact with Brayden? The reason I'm asking is that you know he's been working on this and he even announced that he'll be writing it up soon. So it would seem a bit strange to me to start asking GPT to come up with a solution, if you have not checked in with Brayden beforehand. Now, I understand that pressing the 'currently working on this problem' does not mean that anyone can for always claim dibs on a problem or something. Furthermore, people are of course allowed to work on the same thing independently. But still, I hope you understand where I'm coming from. And maybe in general it would be nice if you could also press the 'currently working on this problem' button, whenever applicable.

No, I haven't. I'm going over all unsolved Erdos problems so it's nothing personal and this is also why clicking the button 'currently working on this problem' doesn't really make sense for me. Also I'm against this kind of problem reserving/booking in general. It's too common for people not to supply the drafts/arguments they claim they have, or do it years after. Again, nothing personal here, and I'm happy to collaborate/share credits with Brayden, Vjeko or other people, whatever is the best possible route here. My goal is simply to look deeper at whatever is unsolved and see where I can push top AI models.

I think there's an unwritten rule about not disrupting the existing mathematical practices that could impact other people. In this case the impact is that Brayden's proof, if it's similar to yours, may not look original anymore. Now your goal to "look deeper at whatever is unsolved and see where I can push top AI models" does not contradict with this but needs to be handled thoughtfully. One way to handle it is to communicate with Brayden first. You can still solve problems in bulk, but the step where you integrate with the community (i.e. the posting) will need to take this into consideration. That's what I think.

I think a natural solution here is to create separate web sites to contain bulk AI-generated proof attempts (such as the ones already linked to from the wiki), and to make a small (but not overwhelming) number of links to such sites from this current site.

The problem with this is that it will push ai generated results to be more and more proprietary. You're seeing them now because people are volunteering to use their subscriptions to share the results. It is unclear to me if the Frontier Labs will make up for the deficit. If you could find a way to properly incentivize people to contribute on your separate website, that would work, otherwise all this effort will just disappear. There is a similar problem happening on open source git repositories. People are contributing mass amounts of real bugs and real vulnerabilities because of AI. Maintainers are complaining about the increased load, but it is nothing but a good problem. We need to fix these real bugs and real vulnerabilities.

I recently wrote on this site about the new impedance mismatch between proof generation and proof digestion. I can offer the following culinary analogy. In an age of caloric scarcity, one would be happy to obtain food from any source available, as long as it has been verified to be edible and non-poisonous. But, once technology enables an age of caloric abundance, the generation of food is no longer the bottleneck, and it is no longer always appreciated to have arbitrarily large amounts of technically edible food dumped on one's plates. Especially if some other human has already promised to cook a meal for us. In short, I think the current level of AI capabilities in math indicate that discussions should now focus more on quality than on quantity. Having thoughtful, carefully prepared discussions (involving both humans and AI) about a handful of Erdos problems would be preferable in my mind to having a large number of shallow discussion threads mostly involving undigested AI-generated proofs. We are here not to push the capabilities of the latest food-generation models, but instead to have a pleasant conversation over dinner.

Thank you for the analogy. I definitely agree that human proofs are better. I try to cut my posting to bona fide new interesting results and remarks where there is no discussion at all (many such problems). I understand this was clearly going to be proved by Brayden, so I'll take no claim for the problem, and if he (or anyone else) wants to use the notes above, feel free too.

From my point of view, nothing bad happened, and Brayden, you, and anyone else with ideas could still collaborate, complete the solution, publish it, etc. Both are understandable: that someone wants to work peacefully on the problem and that someone wants to comment and contribute (with or without the use of an AI). Luckily, everyone wins in a math collaboration; producing at least an $\varepsilon$-better paper is worth adding more human co-authors or AI helpers.

Thank you for writing this. The goal is all about accelerating discovery. This is becoming more critically important, given the numerous problems the world is facing and the role that math will play in helping solve them.

Oh, well, I wouldn't be that dramatic, and I don't think I expressed myself clearly. I also don't believe that the priority should be to make $\varepsilon$-progress in research no matter what. :) I wanted to say that it is a great privilege of Erdős problems that people can discuss both math and authorship soon enough, right here in this forum. This avoids those all-too-common awkward situations where two groups discover they were working independently. In that light, I find Przemek's offer to help a very natural thing to do and in line with common practices in this forum. All scenarios are still possible: that Brayden finds Przemek's note wrong or useless and rightfully writes a paper by themselves; that Brayden finds it useful or decides that further help might be needed; that Brayden kindly offers to work with Przemek, who then kindly says (as they already did) to just go ahead and use the note with no obligations, etc. It is so much easier when there is a natural place to discuss such issues!
Yes, with this erdosproblems-forum we (the community) are really in a comfortable position.

I couldn't agree more - discussion is what matters the most and it's great to have one place to do so. BTW, I just wanted to add my perspective on AI-human interactions in mathematics as I think most tensions stem from one main difference: mode of working. Human mathematics is chiseled and people are very careful not to put out anything even sligthly wrong. This makes the quality of work higher, but largely limits discusssions. On the other hand working with AI is similar to engineering: 70% correct paper is also interesting, because a new generation of models or a different prompt or a human intervention might give 100%. Also LLMs thrive on loose discussions, drafts, general strategies. We're in a discovery mode currently when it comes to AI+math, but I believe in the end it'll all converge to a pleasant new way of working.

I don't think it's dramatic to say that math will help solve problems that the world is facing. Number Theory has a role in quantum physics and quantum computing, which can lead to vital breakthroughs in material science.

I think he satisfied any concerns with the statement "I've run remarks of Vjeko Kovac and Brayden Letwin through GPT-5.4 Pro". This is headline attribution. With that, he has permission to continue. I imagine if they didn't want their results used, they wouldn't have published them. Also, the conjectures and their end solutions are just a minor piece of the puzzle. As many others have mentioned, it's what they imply and what they can further cascade is what really matters. If you look at this more properly, the OP was paying tribute to the efforts of Vjeko Kovac and Brayden Letwin by showing the cascade effect they have triggered by their work. If someone kindly performs the step of feeding it into an AI, that sounds like a favor to the community. Imagine the acceleration that can take place when mathematicians just need to post ideas and things start getting discovered based off those ideas. The only noteworthy etiquette here is clear and fair attribution, something that can not be under emphasized.

I ran standard check but aborted it halfway because there seemed to be multiple issues along the way. The easiest one for me to verify is in Proposition 7, where given $\beta = 1/60$ and $\eta = 1/40$ it does not follow that the exponent $2\beta + 6\eta - 1/2$ is $-1/3$. You should revise it first.
Thanks for the check, will revise accordingly!
Should this problem be characterized as "proved" (see the above comment)? I don't mind it, of course. I am just mentioning because of the analogy with [521].

I think the tools have been in circulation for obtaining a proof for some time now. Following Yakir's paper we may instead control the amount of roots by two logarithmic integrals of radii $\pm 1/n$ from the unit circle (instead of his finer scale which allows him to obtain a more quantitative result in probability). Oren mentions a paper of Nazarov, Nishry and Sodin for which one may obtain quite nice integrability estimates needed for the almost sure estimates. This paper, coupled with a "new" almost-sure central limit theorem motivated by work of Angst and Poly https://arxiv.org/pdf/1912.09928 should allow us to give a proof of the desired result. I will be writing this up soon, but I am busy with final examinations right now.

Here are several rather obvious remarks, partly in analogy with problem 521, and produced before thinking about the problem. :-) 1) I don't think that this problem should be marked as solved, unless another reference is found. Namely, Erdős was precise about what he meant (p.252), and he meant "a strong law", i.e., almost sure convergence of a sequence of random variables:\[ \frac{R'_n}{n/2} \to 1 \ a.s., \]where $R'_n$ counts the number of roots of $\sum_{k=0}^{n}\epsilon_k z^k$ in $|z|\prec 1$ (even though the closed disk $|z|\leq 1$ doesn't make a big difference). Yakir's paper proves\[ \frac{R'_n}{n/2} \to 1 \ \text{in probability}, \]which is also commonly phrased (in the spirit of your formulation) as\[ R'_n = \Big(\frac{1}{2}+o(1)\Big) n \quad\text{with large probability} \]and it is a weaker claim, except for the concrete error of order $O(n^{9/10})$. 2) On the other hand, it is not clear if Erdős meant the coefficients to be $-1,1$ valued coin tosses (i.e., the Rademacher variables) as he emphasizes in the beginning of Section 2) on the same page, or rather the $0,1$ valued coin tosses (i.e., the Bernoulli variables). Strictly speaking he wrote the latter (just as in problem 521), since he constructed $\epsilon_k$ as the $k$-th digit of $t$ taken from the sample space $[0,1)$, by writing $t=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{\epsilon_k(t)}{2^k}$. However, he might have had some identification on mind, or wanted to write $t=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{\epsilon_k(t)+1}{2^{k+1}}$ instead. I am more inclined to think that he meant $-1,1$. 3) In both cases the a.s. claim seems experimentally quite plausible. An easy simulation shows that in the $-1,1$ case a typical trajectory of $R'_n/(n/2)$ looks like this, while in the $0,1$ case it typically looks like this. In both cases it seems that convergence should in fact hold almost surely as well. 4) For the source of the problem Yakir actually cites Hayman's book from 1967. Erdős did in fact ask (p.252) about the weaker claim as well, as you can see in the sentence beginning with "Here I cannot even prove that...". (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
